It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Institutional design of ecolabels: Sponsorship signals rule strength Nicole Darnall* School of Public Affairs, School of Sustainability, Arizona State University, Phoenix, AZ, USA Hyunjung Ji Department of Political Science, University of Alabama, Tuscaloosa, AL, USA Matthew Potoski Bren School of Environmental Science and Management, University of California, Santa Barbara, Santa Barbara, CA, USA Abstract Ecolabels are designed to help consumers identify environmentally superior products and services; however, they are not all created equal. Some ecolabels have strong rules that promote environmental improvements, while others have weaker rules that permit free- riding. Because information about ecolabel design and rule strength is typically not readily available at the point of purchase, con- sumers struggle to differentiate stronger ecolabels from weaker ones. We investigate whether ecolabel sponsorship is a signal that can help consumers distinguish between ecolabels according to the quality of their institutional design. Using data of 189 prominent ecolabels, we ﬁnd that while most ecolabels have basic rules for environmental performance, monitoring, and conformance, the strength of these rules varies across labels according to sponsoring organization. Independent sponsors have the strongest ecolabel rules, followed by governments. Industry sponsored ecolabels have the weakest rule structures. Taken as a whole, these ﬁndings sug- gest that sponsorship may provide important information about whether an ecolabel is designed with rules that effectively condition ﬁrms to promote meaningful environmental improvements. Keywords: design, ecolabel, ecolabel sponsor, environmental performance standard, monitoring and conformance. 1. Introduction Consumers, regulators, environmental groups, and other stakeholders increasingly want companies to offer more en- vironmentally friendly products. However, these same stakeholders are rarely able to observe products’ sustainability attributes directly (Nuttavuthisit & Thøgersen 2017), and often distrust companies’ self-reported sustainability claims (King 2007; Baron 2009, 2010; Harbaugh et al. 2011). Stakeholders, therefore, are reluctant to reward ﬁrms for devel- oping environmentally friendly products unless they have conﬁdence that the sustainability claims are genuine. Firms’ thus develop products that offer fewer environmental beneﬁts than they would otherwise.1 Ecolabels are product seals that, when functioning well, reduce information problems between companies and their stakeholders by providing a credible signal about a product’s superior environmental attributes (Darnall et al. 2016). These product seals have the potential to encourage companies to develop products that improve environmental con- ditions, to satisfy stakeholders, and to create market mechanisms for companies to accrue additional market beneﬁts (Grabosky 2013; Darnall & Aragón-Correa 2014; Fischer & Lyon 2014; van der Ven 2015). However, ecolabels do not always live up to their promise. Weak rules fail to provide sufﬁcient incentives for ﬁrms to improve their environmental performance (e.g. Rivera & De Leon 2004; Darnall & Carmin 2005; Potoski & Prakash 2013a,b). For instance, weak rules do not require speciﬁc environmental targets and data to measure a product’s envi- ronmental performance. They might also lack monitoring criteria, or require few procedures that ensure conformance Correspondence: Nicole Darnall, School of Public Affairs, School of Sustainability, Arizona State University, 411 N Central Avenue, Suite 400, Phoenix, AZ 85004-0687, USA. Ecolabels might still be effective at reducing information prob- lems if stakeholders could distinguish the strong labels from the weak. However, stakeholders often have little direct knowledge about the institutional design of ecolabels2 (Li & van’t Veld 2015) and searching for information is costly (Darnall & Carmin 2005). One path to solving these problems is for consumers and other stakeholders to use information cues (Grabosky 2013) to evaluate ecolabel strength (Jiang et al. 2008). Cues can reduce the information acquisition costs related to assessing the unobserved strength of an ecolabel’s rules. These cues might be obtained from the ecolabel itself, or via other readily available sources, and include the type of organization sponsoring the label (Darnall et al. 2016). We suggest that label sponsorship, which sometimes appears on the label itself, may serve as useful cue for evaluating ecolabels. In this paper, we investigate whether sponsorship provides stakeholders with a useful signal about the rule strength of ecolabels. We consider three types of ecolabel sponsors – industry associations, independent entities, and govern- ments – and suggest that each type of sponsor has different incentives to design more or less stringent ecolabel rules. Industry sponsors have incentives to design ecolabels with weaker institutional requirements in order to offer ﬁrms that display their ecolabel the beneﬁts of stakeholder rewards without incurring the costs associated with environmental im- provements (Fischer & Lyon 2014). By contrast, independent sponsors design labels with stronger rule structures be- cause these sponsors are concerned with ensuring strong environmental performance and are highly dependent on the strength of their ecolabel brand (Reinecke et al. 2012). Government sponsors have competing incentives for the ecolabels they manage. On the one hand they may be more likely to develop more stringent rule standards to achieve the best environmental outcomes as possible, but on the other they may develop weaker standards to attract greater numbers of ﬁrms to display their ecolabels (thus enhancing their political clout) (Darnall & Carmin 2005; Fischer & Lyon 2014; Li & van’t Veld 2015). We analyze the institutional design of 189 international ecolabels, the largest comparative study of ecolabel rules to date. We examine the extent to which these ecolabels incorporate three rule categories: environmental perfor- mance standards, monitoring criteria, and conformance requirements, and then compare these requirements across different sponsors that administer the ecolabels. Our ﬁndings offer evidence that industry sponsored ecolabels have the weakest institutional design, whereas independently sponsored ecolabels have the strongest designs. Government sponsored programs have institutional designs that lie somewhere in-between, although they are closer in form to industry sponsored programs. These ﬁndings suggest that program sponsorship is an important information cue signaling whether an ecolabel is likely to be effective in conditioning ﬁrms to promote improved environ- mental outcomes. 2. Ecolabel design and rule structure Effective ecolabels solve information problems between ﬁrms and their stakeholders (and especially consumers) in that ﬁrms receive recognition for producing environmentally friendly products, and their stakeholders, who are now able to distinguish environmental leaders from laggards, bestow goodwill and market beneﬁts by purchasing ecolabeled products. Based on this promise, ecolabels have proliferated from a dozen worldwide in the 1990s (Delmas et al. 2013) to more than 450 in 2014 (Darnall & Aragón-Correa 2014). However, the mere presence of an ecolabel does not always signal a product’s superior environmental attributes. For instance, the majority of the United States ﬁsh farming industry’s 20 ecolabels have been criticized for failing to distinguish between sustainably produced and conventional ﬁsh (Volpe et al. 2011). Ambiguities such as these have led to skepticism among consumers and other stakeholders about the legitimacy of some ecolabels (Darnall et al. 2012; Delmas et al. 2013). Moreover, while stakeholders typically understand ecolabels in general terms, they often lack speciﬁc understanding of what a particular label might require and how its standards are enforced. For example, Europe’s most widely recognized ecolabel is the European Union (EU) Flower. This label indicates that a product or service has a reduced environmental impact throughout its life cycle, from the extraction of raw material through to production, use, and disposal (European Commission 2016). Although most consumers know that the EU is the ecolabel sponsor, 40 percent of citizens in the United Kingdom report that they do not know the exact meaning of the EU Flower ecolabel (Austgulen 2013), and far fewer understand the label’s formal rules. Darnall et al. © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 439 Page 3: knowledge is even more apparent for lesser-known labels, such as “Global Green Tag Certiﬁed” or “Cradle to Cradle Certiﬁed.” Because searching for information about the strength of ecolabel rules is costly (Darnall & Carmin 2005), con- sumers might be better served by relying on other information cues instead. One potential information cue is the type of organization sponsoring the ecolabel (van der Ven 2015; Darnall et al. 2016). If sponsorship credibly signals the qual- ity of ecolabel rules, ecolabels may be better positioned to help solve information problems between environmentally progressive companies and their stakeholders. Previous research (e.g. Darnall & Carmin 2005; Prakash & Potoski 2006, 2012; Henriques et al. 2013) suggests that strong rule structures are more likely to encourage companies to reduce their environmental impact. While robust rules do not necessarily guarantee that a product will be environmentally superior to competing products, the absence of rule structures almost certainly guarantees an ecolabel’s failure to require participating ﬁrms to improve environmental per- formance. Three types of rules are particularly important: environmental performance standards (Prakash & Potoski 2006), monitoring criteria, and conformance requirements (Darnall & Carmin 2005). An ecolabel’s environmental performance standards deﬁne the environmental performance outcomes products must achieve in order to display the label. One type of performance standard is the requirement that ﬁrms use speciﬁc performance metrics or data for their labeled products. By incorporating environmental performance standards into their business practices, ﬁrms are more likely to meet those standards because metrics enable ﬁrms to measure progress toward a performance goal (Atlee & Kirchain 2006). Another important aspect of an ecolabel’s environmental perfor- mance standard involves requiring ﬁrms to conduct assessments of their environmental performance. Such assess- ments are important because ﬁrms tend to manage what they measure. Similarly, ecolabels that require ﬁrms to achieve ecolabel performance targets over time require commitment to long-term environmental performance expec- tations that help ﬁrms sustain their environmental improvements into the future. A second important category of ecolabel rules is monitoring criteria. Effective monitoring criteria determine whether a product bearing an ecolabel meets the label’s environmental standards (Castka & Corbett 2016). Third party auditing is a particularly important monitoring feature for improving ecolabel efﬁcacy (Deaton 2004; Darnall & Sides 2008; Castka & Corbett 2016). Ongoing audits by independent third parties involve systematic, docu- mented processes for obtaining evidence and determining the extent to which ecolabel criteria are fulﬁlled. Such assessments can encourage continuous improvements over time (Tanner 2000) and bolster the label’s credibility among critical stakeholders (Deaton 2004; Atkinson & Rosenthal 2014; Castka & Corbett 2016). Similarly, third party certiﬁcation goes a step further by indicating that an independent third party has conﬁrmed that a product adheres to an ecolabel’s rule structures. Finally, conformance requirements articulate the processes that a ﬁrm must undertake when its product fails to meet the ecolabel’s environmental performance standards or monitoring criteria (Delmas & Burbano 2011). Conformance requirements include requiring ﬁrms to issue corrective action reports that oblige companies to make speciﬁc procedural changes in order to meet the ecolabel’s environmental performance standards and mon- itoring criteria. By virtue of assessing nonconformance and developing a corrective action remedy, ﬁrms are more likely to follow through and conform to the standard (Darnall & Carmin 2005). In a voluntary setting where legal penalties for nonconformance do not exist, information disclosure is another type of requirement that can ensure ﬁrms’ conformance with program rules (Delmas & Burbano 2011; Lyon & Montgomery 2013). By publicly dis- closing information about the efﬁcacy of the ecolabel, external sources can monitor ﬁrms’ adherence to the pro- gram’s environmental performance standards, thus increasing transparency and reducing opportunities for label misuse. While the theoretical and empirical research clearly indicates that ecolabels need appropriate rule structures in or- der to live up to their policy potential (Bougherara & Grolleau 2005; Castka & Corbett 2016; Dendler 2014), the extent to which ecolabels are designed with strong overall rule structures and whether the strength of overall rule structures differ across sponsors remains uncertain. Assessing the overall rule structure of ecolabels is important because even if an ecolabel has strong monitoring criteria and conformance rules, weak environmental performance standards are likely to lead to the development of ecolabeled products that fail to improve environmental conditions. Similarly, ecolabels with strong environmental standards but weak monitoring criteria or conformance requirements create op- portunities for labeled products to avoid meeting environmental performance standards. However, because ecolabel research has tended to focus narrowly on a single ecolabel (e.g. Brown et al. 2002; Heinzle & Wüstenhagen 2012), it Institutional design of ecolabels N. Comparing strengths of ecolabel rule structures across sponsors is particularly important for consumers because they generally have insufﬁcient information to distinguish the strong ecolabels from the weak. Consumers often have limited knowledge about the rule structure of even the most common ecolabels in the market (Bougherara & Grolleau 2005; Austgulen 2013; Dendler 2014). Sponsorship, therefore, appears to be one feature that stakeholders, and espe- cially consumers, can use to help them differentiate between ecolabels according to their rule strength. 3. Ecolabel design and sponsorship Sponsors put ecolabel rule structures in place. There are three main types of sponsors that administer ecolabels: indus- try associations, independent organizations, and government entities (Fischer & Lyon 2014; Li & van’t Veld 2015). We outline why ecolabel rules are likely to vary across sponsors and why consumers might rely on sponsorship as an infor- mation cue that signals the robustness of an ecolabel’s rules, and hence its effectiveness in promoting positive environ- mental behavior. 3.1. Industry association sponsors Industry associations are formed to promote the interest of member ﬁrms within a particular industrial sector (Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Li & van’t Veld 2015). Industry associations may create their own ecolabels to help market their members’ products (Bratt et al. 2011; Li & van’t Veld 2015). These sponsors have considerable incentive to create ecolabel rules that attract interest from the broadest range of products and member companies (Li & van’t Veld 2015). However, improving a business’s environmental conditions often comes at a cost (Walley & Whitehead 1994; Bernstein & Cashore 2007) and association members are likely to pressure for weaker rule standards (Fischer & Lyon 2014; Li & van’t Veld 2015). Because consumers cannot assess the strength of these ecolabels (Nuttavuthisit & Thøgersen 2017), association members may still beneﬁt from using them. Moreover, because members pay to be part of their association, industry sponsors have a strong incentive to address member concerns and enhance the environmental reputation of their member businesses (Darnall et al. 2010). Instead of promoting genuine environmental improvement, an industry-sponsored ecolabel might therefore provide an opportunity for ﬁrms to symbolically respond to consumers’ preferences for environmentally products by creating the appearance, but not the substance, that products are actually better for the environment. 3.2. Independent sponsors Independent sponsors have incentives to design ecolabels with relatively strong rule structures. Independent spon- sors are not afﬁliated with industry and include environmental non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and standard-setting entities (Carmin et al. 2003). With respect to environmental NGOs, improving the natural envi- ronment is central to their organizational mission, and the vehicle through which they attract support from their own stakeholders. In fulﬁlling their mission, many environmental NGOs serve as societal watchdogs that monitor corporate environmental activities. Other incentives for creating ecolabels with stronger rule structures relate to the fact that environmental NGOs are formed by members and donors who support their organizational mission. Should members or donors believe that the environmental NGO only symbolically supports the objective of improving the natural environment, they are likely to withdraw critical support (Reinecke et al. 2012). Additionally, by promoting strong program rules, environmental NGOs can boost their legitimacy to future funders. Environmental NGOs therefore may have strong incentives to develop ecolabels with rules that are designed to improve ﬁrms’ environmental performance (Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Reinecke et al. 2012; Fischer & Lyon 2014; Li & van’t Veld 2015). Independent standard-setting entities facilitate trade by way of enhancing the value of their brand (Auld 2014). However, brands of any sort must convey credible information in order to be successful (Blackshaw 2008). Credibility hinges on an ecolabel’s ability to provide valid information about the environmental impacts of speciﬁc products (Carmin et al. 2003). In the absence of this credibility, consumers are less likely to use the ecolabel when making their N. Darnall et al. Thus, like environmental NGOs, independent standard-setting organizations have an incentive to develop ecolabels with strong rule structures. 3.3. Government sponsors Government sponsors have incentives to create ecolabels with rule structures that fall between those sponsored by in- dustry associations and independent entities (Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Fischer & Lyon 2014). Government sponsors face competing pressures between improving environmental outcomes and satisfying industry interests (Fischer & Lyon 2014; Li & van’t Veld 2015). On the one hand, internal stakeholders regard government programs that attract a large number of voluntary participants as being successful (Darnall & Carmin 2005). Ecolabels with weaker rule struc- tures are more likely to achieve this objective. On the other hand, weaker rule structures are less likely to lead to mean- ingful environmental improvements, which in the long run can generate mistrust (Nuttavuthisit & Thøgersen 2017) among societal stakeholders. Weak program rules are also incongruent with a government’s mission to improve social conditions. When confronted with this conundrum, governments tend to negotiate and compromise among political interests seeking stronger and weaker rule standards. The outcome is ecolabels with somewhat weaker ecolabel rules than those developed by independent organizations, but stronger rules than industry standards (Fischer & Lyon 2014). Thus, we posit the following hypotheses: Hypothesis 1: Compared to industry sponsored ecolabels, independent and government sponsored ecolabels have stronger rule structures. Hypothesis 2: Compared to government sponsored ecolabels, independently sponsored ecolabels have stronger rule structures. To investigate these hypotheses, we assess the environmental performance standards, monitoring criteria, and con- formance requirements of 189 ecolabels operating worldwide. We examine differences between program sponsors to empirically determine whether variations in the strength of ecolabel rule structures exist between sponsorship catego- ries, and, thus, whether sponsorship could serve as a valid signal that helps stakeholders differentiate between ecolabels according to their rule strength. 4. Data and methods 4.1. Data To assess variations in the institutional designs of ecolabels, we relied on three data sources, the ﬁrst of which was ob- tained from Ecolabel Index. Developed by the B Corporation, Big Room, Ecolabel Index is the largest directory of ecolabels, containing information on the rules of ecolabels that are implemented worldwide. Ecolabel Index assembled its data using an online survey administered to managers working for the ecolabel’s sponsoring organization. These ecolabel managers were identiﬁed by way of online searches using key words. Additionally, Ecolabel Index used a snow- ball technique by asking ecolabel managers whether they knew of other ecolabels that should be included in the list. Ecolabel managers (not identiﬁed by Ecolabel Index or by the snowball technique) could also self-report information via the same online survey at the Ecolabel Index website. By January 2013, Ecolabel Index had compiled program design data for 436 ecolabels. We limited the data by three criteria. First, each ecolabel had to operate in a country that was part of the 34 Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) member nations. These countries represent many of the world’s most advanced nations, possessing stronger institutional settings and higher gross domestic product (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development [OECD] 2014). Additionally, as part of their OECD membership, these countries are required to em- brace the OECD’s goal of reducing global environmental impacts (OECD 2014). A total of 391 ecolabels had primary ofﬁces headquartered within OECD countries. Our second and third criteria for inclusion were that the ecolabels had to be functioning as of July 2014, and that their contact information was available online. A total of 352 ecolabels met our criteria, of which, Ecolabel Index had obtained complete survey data for 87 (24.72 percent) of the ecolabels in the sam- ple. Ecolabel managers were contacted via email and assured that their individual responses would be kept conﬁdential. Non-respondents were sent up to four follow-up email messages. A total of 102 program managers (38.5 percent) responded to our online survey. Coupled with the complete data from Ecolabel Index, we obtained ecolabel design information for 189 (53.7 percent of the 352 ecolabels in our restricted sample), which is by far the largest sample of ecolabels or voluntary programs that have been compared (van der Ven 2015; Darnall & Carmin 2005). Ecolabel managers answered 10 close-ended questions that mirrored the survey data collected by Ecolabel Index. The survey elicited information about each ecolabel’s rule structure. Questions were asked about the presence or ab- sence of aspects of these ecolabel rules, to which ecolabel managers could answer “Yes” (1) or “No” (0). Rules that ad- dressed the environmental performance outcomes that a product must achieve in order to display the label were categorized as environmental performance standards (Prakash & Potoski 2006). Rules that determined whether a prod- uct bearing an ecolabel met the label’s environmental standards (Castka & Corbett 2016) were categorized as monitor- ing criteria. Finally, rules that articulated the processes that a ﬁrm must undertake when its product fails to meet the ecolabel’s environmental performance standards or monitoring criteria (Delmas & Burbano 2011) were categorized as conformance requirements. While it might be easy to assume that all three ecolabel rules could be valued equally, our position is simply that the presence of one rule without the others undermines the label’s ability to perform equivalently against labels that use all three rules. 4.1.1. Environmental performance standards. Our data collection included three questions about each ecolabel’s environmental performance standards. The ﬁrst question drew on prior literature suggesting that having speciﬁc per- formance metrics or data leads to improved performance by enabling ﬁrms to measure progress toward a performance goal (Atlee & Kirchain 2006). We measure whether each ecolabel required ﬁrms bearing their logo to report environ- mental information to the ecolabel managers. Ecolabel managers were asked whether their “ecolabel requires organi- zations that use the label on their products/services to provide speciﬁc metrics or performance data.” Because ecolabel sponsors may not necessarily assess the environmental or performance data that ﬁrms collect, ecolabel managers were asked, “Does your organization assess the environmental/social impacts associated with your ecolabel?” Because ecolabels that require performance targets over time are more likely to encourage ﬁrms to commit to long term environmental performance expectations, a third type of environmental performance standard was whether or not ecolabel users were “required to improve the environmental/social performance of their ecolabeled product over time.” 4.1.2. Monitoring criteria. We utilized two measures of monitoring criteria, the ﬁrst of which focused on audits. Ecolabel managers were asked whether their “ecolabel conducts ongoing audits to determine the extent to which ecolabel criteria are fulﬁlled.” Additionally, ecolabel managers were asked whether or not the ongoing auditor was an independent third party organization. For our second monitoring criteria measure, we determined whether or not the ecolabel required that products bearing their logo undergo external certiﬁcation. Additionally, ecolabel managers were asked, “what entity veriﬁed con- formity to the ecolabel.” Veriﬁcation is the process by which an organization’s products/services are assessed for whether they conform to a set of ecolabel program criteria. Respondents indicated whether veriﬁcation was determined by a third party organization. 4.1.3. Conformance requirements. We assessed conformance requirements by way of two actions: corrective action reports and public reporting. Ecolabel managers were asked whether “your ecolabel requires or recommends organi- zations that use the label on their products/services to issue corrective action reports.” Additionally, ecolabel managers were asked whether they required ecolabel users “to make their product assessments and/or audit reports available publicly.” N. Program sponsorship In its original survey of ecolabel managers, Ecolabel Index asked respondents to indicate whether their ecolabel was developed by an industry association, an independent sponsor, or the government. Sponsorship information was included for all but 12 of these ecolabels. We obtained the missing data for these ecolabels by way of information listed on each ecolabel’s website. Of the 189 ecolabels in our sample, 16.4 percent (n = 31) were sponsored by government, which is statistically similar (14.5 percent, n = 41) to the proportion of government sponsored labels in the population. Independently sponsored labels accounted for 61.9 percent (n = 117) in our sample compared to 55.7 percent in the population (P < 0.01), and 21.7 percent were industry sponsored compared to 29.8 percent in the population (P < 0.01). One pos- sible reason for the underrepresentation of industry sponsored ecolabels in our sample is that industry managers may have been less willing to disclose information about their ecolabel designs unless they believed their ecolabel rules were sufﬁciently strong (Marquis et al. 2016). If so, the industry sponsored ecolabels in our sample may have stronger rules than those in the broader population. By contrast, the overrepresentation of independent sponsors may be a result of greater pressure to increase their visibility, which encourages their willingness to disclose information (Ebrahim 2003). Indeed, during the administration of our survey, independent ecolabel managers tended to provide more information about their labels, regardless of the actual strength of their ecolabel rules. We are therefore less concerned about the overrepresentation of independently sponsored ecolabels in our sample and the consequent possibility of fundamental differences in rule strength between participants and non-participants. In addition to assessing the distribution of ecolabels across sponsors, we also assessed the prevalence of sponsorship identiﬁcation for each of the ecolabels in our sample. Almost two-thirds (63 percent) of independently sponsored and 68 percent of industry sponsored ecolabels provide sponsorship information. By contrast, slightly more than one-third (39 percent) of government sponsored labels provide sponsorship information on their ecolabels. 4.3. Empirical analyses To assess variations in ecolabel design by program sponsor, we used an omnibus Fisher’s exact test (two-tailed). We then utilized Fisher’s exact pairwise comparisons (two-tailed) to draw comparisons between individual program sponsors. Fisher’s exact test is a nonparametric equivalent of the chi-square test and determines statistical differences between two or more categorical variables. It is appropriate to use when frequencies in certain categories are small. 5. Results Table 1 presents the frequencies of the rule structures for all ecolabels in our sample. Overall, a sizable proportion (84 percent) of the ecolabels require that participating companies provide speciﬁc environmental metrics or perfor- mance data. However, fewer (65 percent) actually assess participants’ environmental/social impacts associated with the ecolabel. Just over half (58 percent) of the ecolabels have the more robust standard of requiring participating companies to improve their environmental/social performance over time. We ﬁnd that industry sponsored are as likely as government sponsored ecolabels to require the reporting of speciﬁc environmental metrics or per- formance data (90 percent of government sponsored vs. 93 percent of industry sponsored ecolabels). By contrast, 79 percent of independently sponsored ecolabels have similar requirements (P < 0.10). Table 2 also shows that independent sponsored ecolabels are more likely (P < 0.05) to have stronger monitoring criteria than industry sponsored ecolabels. Sixty-eight percent of government sponsored ecolabels and 64 percent of independent sponsored ecolabels require that labeled products undergo third party ongoing audits to determine the extent to which ecolabel criteria are fulﬁlled. By contrast, 49 percent of industry sponsored ecolabels have this require- ment. The pattern of results is somewhat different for the external certiﬁcation monitoring criteria. Sixty-eight percent of independently sponsored ecolabels require third party certiﬁcation compared to only 55 percent of government sponsored (P < 0.10) and 54 percent (P < 0.05) of industry sponsored ecolabels. Finally, Table 2 shows that independently sponsored ecolabels have stronger conformance requirements. Eighty percent require corrective action reporting, while government sponsored ecolabels require corrective action reports for 65 percent (P < 0.10) of their ecolabels.
